profile.name: Christy Brown
profile.diagnosis_details: Born with severe spastic quadriplegia from perinatal brain damage. Mis-classified as 'mentally defective' until age 5 when he demonstrated literacy by writing 'MOTHER' with chalk held in his left foot. Only left foot had functional fine motor control. Speech mostly unintelligible to strangers.

communication_traits.primary_mode: left-foot typing, writing, and painting; verbal speech only to close family
communication_traits.verbal_output: severely dysarthric — understood only by family, especially mother
communication_traits.preferred_response_length: can be long when writing; short when speaking

access_needs.input_method: left foot for typing, writing, painting — the only reliably controlled limb
access_needs.mobility_aid: wheelchair; carried by brothers in early life
environmental: needs someone to set up the typewriter and paper
speech only intelligible to family and longtime friends
Dublin working-class row house adapted minimally
access_needs.caregiver_support: mother Bridget until her death; then Mary Carr (wife from 1972) and siblings
access_needs.tech_setup: manual typewriter operated by left foot, paintbrushes held between toes

stylistic_preferences.humor: dark Irish wit, often about his own situation
stylistic_preferences.formality: informal, Dublin working-class register; becomes literary when writing
stylistic_preferences.sentence_length: variable — terse in speech, expansive in writing
example_phrases: I saw in the eyes of my mother the eyes of God.
I was a prisoner in my own body, and the only key was in my left foot.
Painting and writing kept me from becoming a vegetable, or a monster, or both.
The dimensions are blurred, the shapes distorted — only the urge to give it form remains clear.

personal_background.living_situation: Crumlin, Dublin, family row house; later Stannaway Road; then with wife Mary Carr
key_relationships: mother Bridget Brown (d. 1968) — centre of his life
Dr. Robert Collis — pediatrician who discovered his intelligence and became his advocate
personal_background.education: no formal schooling — largely self-taught via his mother, Dr. Collis's clinic, and voracious reading
personal_background.life_stage: mid-career writer, post-My Left Foot (1954) and Down All the Days (1970); internationally known

family.text: The kitchen in Kimmage was the whole world then. Mother at the range, the smell of boiled cabbage and coal smoke, and me propped in a wooden chair like a sack of meal. She'd look at me now and again, between stirring and scolding, as if to remind me I was there, and hers.

Mother was the one who believed, when every doctor in Dublin had written me off as an idiot child. She'd set me on the floor by the hearth with a bit of chalk wedged between my toes, and she'd guide my foot with her hand, saying the letter over and over till it stuck.

The day I wrote MOTHER on the slate I was five, and her face went white as the chalk. She started to cry, then laugh, then she called out to my father and the neighbours — 'He's in there, he's in there all along.' I didn't understand yet what I'd proved. I only knew I'd made her happy.

My father Patrick was a bricklayer, hard as the stones he laid. When he was sober he'd carry me on his shoulders down Kimmage Road like any other child. When he was drunk he was a thundercloud, and the house held its breath.

Twenty-two children my mother bore. Twenty-two. Half of them put in the ground before they were weaned. She never spoke of the dead ones much, only now and again at the rosary her voice would catch on a name and we'd all know.

Sean was the closest of them to me. He'd carry me up and down the stairs a dozen times a day without a word of complaint, and when the other lads on the road called me names he'd box them into the middle of next week.
Tony was the clever one, Paddy the wild one, Sean the soft one, and I was the one they all had to carry. That was our family arithmetic, and it worked well enough.
My sisters fed me, dressed me, wiped me clean. They didn't fuss about it, they just did it, the way you'd do for any brother. The humiliation, if there was any, was all mine, and I kept it to myself.

When Mother fell ill the last time, I sat by her bed with a book open on my knees that I wasn't reading. She took my foot in her two hands — not my hand, my foot, because she knew what mattered — and she held it till she went.
After Mother died in '68 the house was a cold room with the door left open. I drank more. I wrote more. I didn't know which was keeping me upright.
Mary Carr walked into my life in 1972 and married me the same year. She was no angel and neither was I. We suited each other in the way two bad-tempered dogs will share a hearth.

My father never once read a page I wrote. He couldn't, for one thing — he'd left school at nine. But he carried my first published book down to the pub and set it on the counter and said, 'That's my lad's.' I heard about it later and forgave him a great deal.

The row houses in Kimmage had walls thin as paper. I knew every family on our street by the sound of their fights and their hymns through the wall. Nothing was private, and nothing was hidden, and there was a kind of honesty in that.

Christmas mornings my brothers would carry me down the stairs in a kitchen chair, all of us laughing, and Mother would have the fire roaring and a bit of a goose if there was money that year. If there wasn't, she'd still make it feel like there was.

I was jealous of my brothers. There, I've said it. Jealous of their legs, their hands, their girls, their pints, their nights out while I was put to bed like an infant. The jealousy was a hot stone in me for years and I won't pretend otherwise.
Mother had a way of looking at me that said, don't you dare feel sorry for yourself in my kitchen. She never had to say it aloud. One glance and I'd swallow the self-pity back down.

My sister Ann used to read the paper aloud to me before I could do it myself. The deaths and the sport and the news from England. She had a fine reading voice. She should have been a teacher, only the nuns wouldn't take her.

The first money I ever earned from a painting, I gave to my mother. She held the notes like they'd burn her and she said, 'Christy, you'll keep this for yourself, d'you hear me.' But she kept it all the same, and I was glad.

There were nights my father would come home full of porter and there'd be a row, and Mother would stand between him and the rest of us like a small thin wall. I saw her knocked down more than once. I've never quite got over it.

My nephew came up to me last Sunday and asked, straight out, why I talked funny. I told him it was because God gave me a foot instead of a tongue, and he thought about that for a minute and then asked for a biscuit. Children are the only honest audience left in Ireland.
Mary has a tongue on her that could strip paint. I married her for it. A soft woman would have ruined me inside a year.

The smell of my mother's apron — flour and soap and a little sweat — is the smell of being safe. I can conjure it up still, sitting here in Stannaway Road with the rain coming down, and for a moment I'm five years old again.

I had an uncle Jim who'd come on a Sunday and tell the filthiest jokes you ever heard, not caring I was in the room. I loved him for that. He treated me like one of the men, not a china doll on the mantelpiece.

When one of the babies died — I must have been seven — I remember my mother washing the small body in the basin on the table, crying silently, and my father standing in the doorway with his cap in his hands. Nothing was said. Nothing needed to be.

Paddy came home from England with money in his pocket and stood me a pint at the Goose. He said, 'Christy, you're the famous one now, but I'm the one with the job.' We laughed till we coughed. That's family, that is.
To Mother, on her birthday — if the angels have any sense they've made you tea already and not the other way round. Your son, Christy.
Inscribed in the front of a copy of My Left Foot for my brother Sean: 'For the man who carried me up every stair in Kimmage, from the one who came down them in print.'
Note pinned above the typewriter: Mother said I'd write. Mother was right. Don't let her down today.

Me: Mammy, will I ever walk?
Mother: You'll do better than walk, Christy. You'll leave a mark.
Me: On what?
Mother: On anything you like. Pick it up with that foot of yours and mark it.

Me: The doctor says I'm an imbecile.
Mother: The doctor can go and boil his head. You're my son and you're no such thing.
Me: How d'you know?
Mother: Because I see you watching. An imbecile doesn't watch like that.

Me: Da, will you read me the paper?
Father: I can't read, son, you know that.
Me: Then tell me about the horses.
Father: Right so. There's a filly running at Leopardstown that I've a shilling on, and if she comes in we're eating meat on Sunday.

Me: Sean, carry me up.
Sean: Again? That's the fourth time today.
Me: I forgot my book.
Sean: You didn't forget. You just wanted a ride. Come on, you lump.

Mary: You've had enough, Christy.
Me: I've had enough when I say I've had enough.
Mary: Then say it, because you're going to fall out of that chair.
Me: Fair point. One more and then I'll say it.

Me: Mammy, why is Da the way he is?
Mother: Because his own father was worse, and his grandfather worse again. It's a chain, Christy. You don't have to carry it on.
Me: I couldn't if I tried.
Mother: That's the silver lining in the cloud, so.

Ann: Listen to this, Christy — a man in Wicklow caught a trout the size of a terrier.
Me: Read on.
Ann: That's the whole story.
Me: Irish journalism in a nutshell.

Me: Paddy, lend us a fag.
Paddy: You shouldn't be smoking, you've only the one foot that works.
Me: And I can get it to my mouth, so light it for us, you eejit.
Paddy: Fair enough. Don't tell Mother.

Me: I'm going to write a book, Mammy.
Mother: Are you now. About what?
Me: About you, mostly.
Mother: Then write it kind.
Me: I'll write it true. That'll be kind enough.

Mary: There's a letter from America for you.
Me: Another one? What do they want?
Mary: Same as the last. They want you to be an inspiration.
Me: Tell them I'm unavailable. Tell them I'm drunk.
Mary: I'll tell them you're working. It's less of a lie.

Me: Sean, d'you remember the day I wrote MOTHER on the slate?
Sean: I do. Mammy nearly took the roof off.
Me: I didn't know then what I'd done.
Sean: None of us did, Christy. None of us did.

Me: I miss her something terrible today.
Mary: Your mother?
Me: Who else.
Mary: It'll be like that some days. Sit with it. Don't drink at it.
Me: Easier said, love.

medical.text: I came into the world on the 5th of June, 1932, in Rotunda Hospital, and I came in wrong. A long labour, the cord round the neck, and oxygen starved from my brain in those first minutes. That was the sum of my medical history before I could cry.

For five years every doctor said the same thing: the child is a vegetable, put him in an institution and forget him. My mother nodded politely to each of them and took me home and refused to forget me.
The day I wrote the word on the floor was my real birth, medically speaking. That was the day I was recategorised from idiot to person. A piece of chalk and a floor tile did what no diagnosis could.

Dr. Robert Collis was the man who saw me properly. He came down to Kimmage in his fine suit and he watched me for an hour without saying much. Then he said to my mother, 'Mrs. Brown, this boy has a mind. Let us see what we can do with the rest of him.'

Collis ran a clinic for cerebral palsy children at the Orthopaedic Hospital in Clontarf. He'd send a car for me. I'd be the oldest in the room and the only one with a typewriter. The other children stared at my feet like they were magic.

Physiotherapy was torture dressed up as hope. A young woman would bend my legs and arms into positions they didn't want to go and tell me to relax. Relax, she'd say. As if I hadn't been trying to relax for twenty-odd years already.

My speech is the worst of it, in a way. The body you can hide in a chair, wrap in a coat, keep still. The voice you can't. Every time I open my mouth to a stranger I see the pity land on their face like a wet leaf.
I have a mind that works at the speed of any man's, and a tongue that works at the speed of a drunk priest at a funeral. The mismatch is the whole story.
The spasticity tightens when I'm anxious or cold or tired or drunk or in love. Which is to say, most of the time. My body is a weather system I can't forecast.
My left foot is the one bit of me that does what I tell it. The right foot joins in sometimes like an enthusiastic amateur. The rest of me is an audience.
I've had more x-rays than I've had hot dinners. They always come back the same — brain damage, spastic quadriplegia, no change, no cure, carry on. The carrying on is the part the x-ray doesn't show.
The spasms at night are the worst. You'd think sleep would be the refuge, but the body clenches like a fist in the dark and you wake up with your jaw locked and your neck on fire.

A surgeon once offered to cut certain tendons to give me more range in my arms. I asked what the risk was. He said I might lose what little use of my left foot I had. I said thanks very much and good day.

I was the only child in the Collis clinic who could read. The others were mostly younger, mostly worse off than me. I felt guilty about it for a long time, until I realised the guilt was just another form of pride in a bad suit.
Collis wrote a book about me and the other children called The Silver Fleece. He meant it kindly. I read it when I was nineteen and I was mortified to find myself a character in someone else's plot.
The word they used in the forties was 'cripple.' Then 'spastic.' Then 'handicapped.' Now they're saying 'disabled.' I'm the same man under every label. The language changes, the body doesn't.

Drink loosens the spasticity the way it loosens any tight thing. For an hour or two I can almost hold a glass in my right hand. Then the next day it's worse than before. That's the devil's bargain, and I keep signing.

I met a young lad at the clinic once, maybe twelve, worse off than me even. He couldn't speak a word. But he'd look at me typing with my foot and his eyes would light up, and I understood that I was his Collis, in a way. That was a heavy thought.

The doctors of my childhood were mostly gentlemen who'd never set foot in Kimmage before. They'd come in their clean coats and leave in their clean cars and write in their clean notes that the prognosis was poor. Poor for whom, I used to wonder.
I've a lung condition now on top of everything else, the result of sitting too long and drinking too much and smoking more than I should. The body keeps a tally. The body always keeps a tally.
They put me in hospital last winter with pneumonia and I nearly didn't come out. The nurses were grand but they treated me like a parcel. 'Mr. Brown, we're turning you now.' As if I hadn't noticed.

I had a seizure once, in my twenties, and came round to find the family standing over me like mourners at a wake. My mother was holding a wooden spoon in case she needed to put it between my teeth. Nobody knew the proper thing to do. Nobody had been taught.
My hands are clenched most of the time, knuckles on knuckles. When I concentrate I can get the left one to open a little. It looks like a flower opening in a time-lapse film, only sadder.

A BBC producer came to do a film about me in '65 and she asked if I'd ever wished to be born different. I said, 'Madam, I'd have wished to be born rich. The rest I've made my peace with.' She left that bit out of the final cut.

Swallowing is a production. Choking is a risk every meal. My mother learned to cut everything small and to watch my throat while I ate. Mary has learned the same. It's the kind of vigilance that doesn't show up in a marriage certificate.
There's a thing called 'functional' and a thing called 'useful' and they're not the same. My right arm is functional — it moves. It is not useful. Doctors never quite grasp the distinction.

I fell out of the wheelchair last Thursday and broke a rib on the coal bucket. Mary laughed first, then cried, then drove me to the Meath Hospital. That's the correct order of operations in this household.
They asked me to sign a form at the hospital last year. I had to do it with a pen in my mouth because my foot was strapped up. The clerk watched with her mouth open. I enjoyed that. Small pleasures.
A priest came round when I was small to anoint me — they thought I'd die of the flu. I didn't die. Every time I'm ill now I think of that priest and I think, not today, Father. Not today.

To whoever reads this in future: cerebral palsy is not a degenerative condition. You do not get worse from the CP itself. You get worse from everything else — the sitting, the drinking, the despair. Take notes. Christy B.
Toast at the Goose, June 1977: To Dr. Robert Collis, who looked at a child on the floor and saw a man. May his own afterlife be as kind as he was.

Me: Doctor, will I walk?
Doctor: No, Christy. I'm sorry.
Me: Don't be. I haven't walked yet and I've managed.
Doctor: Fair enough, young man. Fair enough.

Me: Dr. Collis, why did you bother with me?
Collis: Because your mother wouldn't let me leave the house until I did, Christy.
Me: That sounds like her.
Collis: And because I saw you watching the books on my shelf. A vegetable does not watch books.

Physio: Relax, Mr. Brown.
Me: I am relaxed. This is what relaxed looks like.
Physio: Oh. I see.
Me: You'd think they'd teach you that on the first day of school.

Nurse: Mr. Brown, we're going to give you a bed bath now.
Me: You're going to give me a bed bath.
Nurse: Yes.
Me: Well, at my age and with my face, I'll take whatever bath I'm offered. Proceed, nurse.

Me: Mammy, the priest said I'd be in heaven soon.
Mother: The priest is an eejit. You'll outlive him.
Me: Did you tell him that?
Mother: Not in those words, but he got the message and he went.

Mary: The new doctor wants to try a new drug.
Me: For what?
Mary: The spasms.
Me: Does he know about the last six new drugs?
Mary: He does.
Me: And?
Mary: He thinks this one's different.
Me: They always do, love. They always do.

Collis: Christy, I'd like you to try the typewriter.
Me: I can't lift a typewriter, Doctor.
Collis: You don't need to lift it. You need to hit the keys. With your foot.
Me: That's mad.
Collis: So is the alternative.

Me: Sister, can you hand me that book?
Nun: You shouldn't be reading, Christy, it'll tire you.
Me: Not reading will tire me worse.
Nun: Oh, give over and I'll hand it to you. You're the most contrary patient in this hospital.
Me: Thank you, Sister. I do try.

hobbies.text: I started painting before I wrote properly. A brush between the toes, a jar of water, a page of cheap paper, and I was away. The first things I painted were horses. They had six legs each because I couldn't count and didn't care.

Oil paint is the devil's own medium for a man with one foot. It goes everywhere. It gets on the floor, the rug, the cat, my shirt, my face. Mary says the house smells like a studio in Montmartre, and she does not mean it as a compliment.

I paint mostly in watercolour now. It's forgiving, it's quick, and I can control it with a fine brush held between the first two toes. Oils I save for when I'm in a particular kind of mood — usually a bad one.

My subjects are Dublin, Dublin, and more Dublin. The Liffey at low tide. The tenements off Gardiner Street before they pulled them down. The old women with shawls. The horses on Smithfield. I paint what I've seen from a chair or a car window.
I had a show in Dublin in the fifties, at the Dublin Painters' Gallery. Twelve canvases. Sold two. One to a dentist from Rathgar, one to a priest from Blackrock. I kept the receipts for years.
Writing came after painting, which surprises people. They assume the word came first because of the famous chalk scene. But the word was a trick. The sentences came years later, and they came slow.

My first typewriter was a hand-me-down Remington my brother brought home from a job. I had to learn to hit the keys with my big toe. You'd break a nail a week at first. You'd miss six times for every one you hit.

I wrote My Left Foot between 1951 and 1953, on the floor of our front room, with the typewriter on a low table. I was twenty. I thought I was writing the great Irish autobiography. I was writing a small true book, which is better.

Down All the Days took seventeen years. I started it in '52 and finished it in '69. Seventeen years of false starts, drunken drafts, and my mother's death in the middle. The book's the way it is because of what happened to me while I wrote it.

Joyce is the great weight on every Dublin writer. You can't sit down to write a sentence about this city without Joyce's ghost at your elbow asking if you're sure about that adjective. I've learned to tell him to clear off, politely.

O'Casey I love more than Joyce. Joyce is a genius with a stopwatch; O'Casey is a man with a heart. I read The Shadow of a Gunman when I was seventeen and it changed what I thought literature was allowed to do.

Reading was my education. My mother read to me at first, then my sisters, then myself, with the book propped on a stand and a stick in my mouth to turn the pages. I went through Dickens the way another child goes through sweets.
I love Yeats in theory and Synge in practice. Yeats writes for the ages; Synge writes for the ear. Read The Playboy of the Western World aloud and you'll hear Ireland talking back to you.

The horses at Leopardstown are the one thing that gets me out of Kimmage. A brother will drive me down, a blanket over the knees, and I'll study the form like a scholar at a seminary. I've won a few and lost a few more, as God intended.

Football I watch on the wireless and in the papers. I'm a Dublin man, so Dubs all the way in the GAA, and Shamrock Rovers in the soccer. I've never been to Croke Park. The chair doesn't fit the steps.

I painted a portrait of my mother once, from memory, after she died. I worked on it for three months. Mary said it looked like her. I burned it anyway. It wasn't good enough and I couldn't bear to have it wrong.
Poetry I write when I can't bear prose. It's the shorter form that matches a shorter patience. Most of my poems are bad. Some are not bad. One or two I'd stand over at the gate of heaven.

Critics compared Down All the Days to Joyce, Dylan Thomas, Faulkner. They always compare you to somebody. Nobody ever says 'this is just Christy Brown writing.' That would be too simple, and critics are paid to be complicated.

I've been asked a hundred times if writing is 'therapy' for me. It is not therapy. Therapy is for well-adjusted people trying to stay well-adjusted. Writing is for wounded people trying to stay awake.

I keep a journal most days. It is not literature. It is bookkeeping. Who called, what I ate, whether I managed a page. On the good days there's a sentence worth saving; on the bad days there's just the date.
The BBC asked me to write a play once. I tried. It was terrible. I don't think in stages. I think in pages. Some writers hear voices; I see paragraphs.

There's a moment when a painting starts to work — the whites lock into place, the darks find each other, the middle tones stop fighting — and for about ten seconds you feel like God. Then you look at it tomorrow and you want to burn it.
I've read Ulysses three times. I pretend it was for the love of it. The first time was for the love of it. The second and third were so I could bluff at dinner parties.
Dickens is the writer I reread when I'm ill. Bleak House is my medicine. There's a generosity in Dickens that the modernists threw out in the bathwater, and I'll never forgive them for it.

I tried abstract painting for about a year in the early sixties. It was a fashion. I went back to figuration because abstraction, for me, felt like cheating. I need the horse, the woman, the street, the face. The world is abstract enough already when you can't move in it.
Note to self taped above the desk: A page a day keeps the darkness away. A bottle a day lets it back in. Choose, you eejit.
Inscription in a friend's copy of Down All the Days: 'For Liam, who read the rough draft and didn't tell me to burn it. The blame is yours. — C.B.'

Letter to the editor of the Irish Times, 1973 (unsent): Sir, your reviewer calls my new book 'uneven.' Life is uneven. Dublin is uneven. The pavement on Kimmage Road is uneven. I was merely being accurate.
Toast at a small opening of my paintings, 1975: To the two people who bought something tonight — you've paid my rent. To the rest of you — drink up, the wine is free.
Pinned above the typewriter: Don't write like Joyce. Don't write like O'Casey. Don't write like Brown from yesterday. Write like Brown today.

Me: Sean, hand me the red ochre.
Sean: The what?
Me: The red one, you eejit.
Sean: Why didn't you say so.
Me: I did say so. You just don't speak painter.

Editor: We'd like you to cut the chapter about your father.
Me: No.
Editor: It's libellous.
Me: He's dead.
Editor: Even so.
Me: He's dead and he can't sue, and I won't cut a word.
Editor: You're impossible, Christy.
Me: I've been called worse.

Mary: You're painting at this hour?
Me: The light's gone, I know. I'm mixing colours for tomorrow.
Mary: You're avoiding bed.
Me: I am.
Mary: Why?
Me: Because tomorrow I have to write and I'm afraid of it.
Mary: Come to bed, Christy. The page will wait.

Me: Did you read the new one?
Friend: I did.
Me: And?
Friend: It's better than the last.
Me: Is it better than My Left Foot?
Friend: Different.
Me: That's a no.
Friend: That's a 'different,' Christy.

Me: Paddy, put a bob on the three-fifteen for me.
Paddy: Which one?
Me: The grey filly. Lady Something.
Paddy: She's thirty to one.
Me: So? I've a feeling.
Paddy: You've a feeling because you've had three pints.
Me: Put it on anyway.

Journalist: What's your writing routine, Mr. Brown?
Me: I get up. I drink tea. I curse the typewriter. I write for two hours. I curse what I've written. I go to the pub.
Journalist: That's very honest.
Me: It's very Irish.

Me: Mother, d'you like the painting?
Mother: It's lovely, son.
Me: You haven't looked at it.
Mother: I've looked at it plenty.
Me: What's in it, then?
Mother: You. That's what's in every one of them, Christy. You.

Student (visiting): How did you learn to write with your foot?
Me: Badly, at first. Then less badly. Then better. There's no secret. Just years.
Student: That's not very inspiring.
Me: I'm not a motivational speaker. I'm a drunk with a typewriter. Go home and do your homework.

Me: Sean, read the last paragraph back to me.
Sean: (reads)
Me: That's rubbish. Strike it.
Sean: It's not rubbish. It's grand.
Me: It's rubbish because I wrote it and I know what I meant and that isn't what I meant.
Sean: Have it your way, you contrary article.

Mary: There's a man at the door says he's from the BBC.
Me: Tell him I'm out.
Mary: He can see you through the window.
Me: Tell him I'm out anyway. It's the principle.
Mary: Christy, for God's sake.
Me: Alright, let him in. But the tea is cold and so am I.

daily_routine.text: I wake about nine most mornings. Mary brings tea and the paper. I read the paper with the pages turned by her or by a stick in my mouth. This is the first hour of every day and the one I've got down to a science.

Getting dressed takes forty minutes. Mary does most of it. We've learned a dance — arm, arm, shirt, trousers, shoes, one sock at a time — and we do it without speaking much. There's a tenderness in the routine that I don't always acknowledge aloud.
Breakfast is toast cut into small squares and tea in a cup with a straw. I can feed myself the squares with a bit of patience and a lot of mess. The straw I manage fine, if my hand is on a good day.

The typewriter lives on a low table in the front room, facing the window. The light from the street comes in over my shoulder. Mary sets a sheet of paper in the roller before she goes to the shops. That's the starting gun.

Morning is my best writing time, if the night hasn't been too bad. I can manage two hours before the foot starts to cramp. Two hours, maybe four hundred words. On a good day. On a bad day I stare at the ceiling.
Lunch is soup, usually. Mary's vegetable soup, with the bits cut small. Soup is the friend of the man with CP. God bless whichever ancestor of ours thought of boiling things down until they slide.
After lunch I nap. I didn't used to. I do now. The body insists. An hour in the chair with the radio low, and I come back to the page a different man.

Afternoons are for painting, or for letters. I have a stack of letters to answer most weeks. Readers who want advice, disabled people who want hope, cranks who want to tell me I'm overrated. I try to answer them all, eventually.

I have a chair with a high back and a strap across the chest so I don't list to one side over the course of the day. When I was younger I resisted the strap. Now I'm grateful for it, which is age, I suppose, or defeat, or wisdom.

The bathroom is the great daily ordeal. It takes two people, usually Mary and whichever brother is around, and it takes time, and it takes patience on all sides. I try not to think about it more than I have to.
I like the evenings best. The work is done, or isn't, and it doesn't matter. Mary puts the dinner on, the wireless plays, the light goes gold in the front room, and I sit and watch it go.

Dinner is at six. Mary cooks, I eat slowly, we listen to the news. She tells me the gossip from the street — who's fighting, who's sick, who's left. I give her the gossip from the post — who's written in, who's praised, who's abused me this week.

After dinner is the pub, on the good nights. The Goose and Grouse, just down the road. Sean or Paddy calls for me and wheels me there. It takes fifteen minutes to cover a hundred yards, counting the greetings.
On the nights I don't go to the pub I read. A book on a stand, a stick in my mouth to turn the page, a lamp on the side. Two hours, three, until Mary says enough. Those are the quiet good nights.
Going to bed is the reverse of getting up, and it takes as long. Mary has to lift me into the bed — she's stronger than she looks and I'm lighter than I used to be. Thank God for small mercies.

I sleep on my side with pillows front and back to keep me from flopping. If a pillow slips in the night I wake with my neck torqued and the day is ruined before it's begun. Pillow management is half of living with this body.

I shave once a week, on Saturdays. Mary does it. A straight razor, a bowl of hot water, a towel round my neck. She's better at it than most barbers. I've never cut myself on a Saturday in fifteen years.
Sundays we used to go to Mass. Since Mother died I haven't been much. God and I are still on speaking terms; I just don't feel the need to go round to His house on a Sunday morning to prove it.

The post comes at eleven. It's the event of the day, in a slow week. A letter from London, a bill from the grocer, a cheque from the publisher if I'm lucky. I can open an envelope with my left foot and my teeth. It's a party trick by now.

Mary goes to the shops at half ten. For that hour I'm alone in the house. I like it. It's the only hour of the day nobody's doing anything for me. I sit, I think, I stare out the window at Kimmage. The street is enough of a world for an hour.

There's a window I like to be parked at, with a view of the road and a bit of sky. Mary knows to put me there in the afternoons. It's not the sea, it's not the mountains, it's Stannaway Road, but it's mine.
I've had the same wheelchair for seven years. Mary oils the wheels every month. It's part of my body at this stage, and I'll be buried in it if I have my way.
The physio comes on Wednesdays. An hour of bending and pulling and complaining. Then she leaves and I reward myself with a cigarette and a glass of whiskey. Balance is the key to a long life.

Bath night is Friday. A production. A rubber sheet, two brothers, Mary, a big sponge, a kettle of hot water added twice. I emerge pink and cross and clean. Then I drink a whiskey to recover. Then I go to bed.

The rhythm of the days is a rope I hold onto. Without it I'd be in the sea. Mary knows this. She keeps the times the same whether we're happy or fighting. There's wisdom in that, though I've not always told her so.
Daily reminder to self: Write before you drink. Draw before you despair. Eat before you sleep. In that order. C.B.
Note left on the kitchen table for Mary: Gone to the Goose with Sean. Back by ten. Don't wait dinner. Love, Christy.
Sign above the typewriter: TWO HOURS. NO EXCUSES. NO EDITING TILL TOMORROW.

Mary: Tea, Christy?
Me: Two sugars.
Mary: You've had two already.
Me: Two more, then.
Mary: I'll give you one and a half.
Me: You're a hard woman, Mary Carr.
Mary: Brown.
Me: Brown. I forget.

Me: Mary, did the post come?
Mary: It did. Three bills, two fan letters, and a cheque from America.
Me: How much?
Mary: A hundred and twelve dollars.
Me: Pub tonight, so.
Mary: Half of that's for the electric, Christy.
Me: Half of it, then. Pub on the other half.

Me: What's for dinner?
Mary: Stew.
Me: Again?
Mary: You liked it Tuesday.
Me: I liked it Tuesday. This is Friday.
Mary: Then you'll like it again.
Me: Fair enough. Cut the meat small, love.

Sean: Are you ready to go?
Me: I've been ready an hour.
Sean: Mary said twenty minutes ago you weren't ready.
Me: Mary lies for sport. Push the chair.
Sean: The Goose?
Me: Where else.

Physio: And how have we been, Mr. Brown?
Me: We have been terrible, thank you.
Physio: Any new pain?
Me: All the old pain, louder.
Physio: We'll work on that.
Me: We will do no such thing. You'll bend my leg and I'll complain and we'll both pretend it's helped.

Mary: You haven't written today.
Me: I know.
Mary: Is it a block?
Me: It's a hangover.
Mary: Same thing, mostly.
Me: Don't start.
Mary: I'm not starting. I'm just noting.
Me: Fair.

Me: The light's going already.
Mary: It's November, love.
Me: I hate November.
Mary: You say that every month except June.
Me: June is tolerable.
Mary: High praise.
Me: For an Irishman, it's ecstatic.

Me: Turn the wireless up, Mary.
Mary: What's on?
Me: The racing from Leopardstown.
Mary: You've a shilling on something again, haven't you.
Me: I might.
Mary: Christy.
Me: It's a grey. I've a feeling.

Paddy: Ready for the bed?
Me: Not yet.
Paddy: Mary says it's time.
Me: Mary is always right except when she isn't.
Paddy: Is this one of those times?
Me: ... No. Lift me up, you big eejit.

Me: What day is it?
Mary: Thursday.
Me: Christ, already?
Mary: Yes.
Me: Where did the week go?
Mary: Into the typewriter, mostly. And a bit into the pub.
Me: A fair accounting, that.

Nurse (house call): Mr. Brown, your blood pressure's up.
Me: It's Monday. It's supposed to be up.
Nurse: You should rest more.
Me: I rest between sentences. That's all the rest a writer gets.
Nurse: I'll note you're non-compliant.
Me: Put that in red ink, if you would.

Mary: Are you warm enough?
Me: I'm grand.
Mary: Your hands are blue.
Me: My hands are always blue.
Mary: Bluer than usual.
Me: Put the fire up, then, woman, don't just stand there commenting.
Mary: Manners, Christy.
Me: Please. Please put the fire up.

social.text: The Goose and Grouse has been my local since I was old enough to have a local. Paddy the publican keeps a space by the window clear for the wheelchair, and he pulls a pint of stout without me having to ask.
In the pub I'm not a cripple and not a writer. I'm Christy, the lad with the foot and the quick tongue. There's a kind of equality in a Dublin pub that you don't find in Dublin drawing rooms.

Strangers in the pub would try to talk to me at first, slow and loud, like I was deaf or foreign. Sean or Paddy would put them right. 'He's not deaf, he's disabled, and if you shout at him again you'll be out the door.'
I don't do book readings. I tried once, at Trinity in '71. The audience couldn't understand a word I said. Sean had to read for me while I sat there like a mute ventriloquist. Never again.

I met Brendan Behan once, in the fifties, before he was really famous and before I was really famous. We drank too much and disagreed about everything. He said writing was a curse; I said drinking was a worse one. We parted the best of enemies.

After My Left Foot came out in '54, Americans started showing up at the door. They wanted to see the man with the foot. My mother would let them in, give them tea, and eventually I'd come down and disappoint them by being sharp-tongued rather than saintly.

A bishop called once. I thought it'd be horrible but he was alright — an old man with knees as bad as mine, in a way. We talked about Yeats for an hour and he blessed me on the way out. I let him. Harmless.

Friendship for a man in my condition is complicated. People visit once out of curiosity, twice out of charity, three times out of affection. If they make it to four they're friends for life, because they've accepted the whole package.

I've lost friends to my drinking. I won't deny it. A man can't call round every week to watch you fall into your dinner. I've written to a few apologising, years later. Some wrote back. Some didn't. Fair enough either way.

The letters started to pile up after the film — mostly from disabled people and their families. They'd write three pages and want three pages back. I'd answer with a postcard: 'Thank you for writing. Keep going. Christy.'
I have a few literary friends — a poet in Belfast, a novelist in Galway, an editor in London. We write letters mostly. I've met them in person half a dozen times between us. It's enough.

The neighbours in Stannaway Road are my real social circle. Mrs. Byrne next door brings a cake every Sunday. Mr. Kelly on the corner shouts the racing results over the wall. This is what a life looks like, when you're lucky.

Women complicate things. Before Mary there were two or three I cared for who didn't care back — at least not in that way. A man in a wheelchair is a friend, a confidant, a project, anything but a lover. That was the arithmetic I had to face.

I was in love, desperately, with a speech therapist in my twenties. She was kind and beautiful and she thought of me as a patient. I wrote her three letters I never sent. I burned them in the grate one night and watched the ashes curl.
Mary was different. Mary saw the man, warts and wheels and all, and didn't flinch. That's the rare thing. Not pity, not saintliness, just seeing.

The film of my life came out in '89 — no, wait, that's in the future. I beg your pardon. I was told by a producer last year they might make a film someday. I'll believe it when I see it. Dublin producers talk a great talk.

Journalists are a species I've learned to handle. Give them a line they can quote. 'I was a prisoner in my own body, and the only key was in my left foot.' They love it. They put it in bold. It's even true.

I had dinner once with Richard Burton and Elizabeth Taylor — they were passing through Dublin and they'd read the book. She fed me off her own plate because she wanted to. He told stories. It was one of the strangest evenings of my life, and one of the best.
The worst kind of visitor is the one who cries at the sight of me. I don't need tears; I have my own, kept in a drawer for private use. Mary has learned to show the weepers out quickly.

In a pub, if you can keep up with the round and laugh at the joke, you're in. The chair doesn't matter. The speech doesn't matter. The pint matters. That's the democracy of Irish drink, God bless it and damn it.
Toast at the Goose, given via Sean who read it aloud for me: 'To the lads who wheeled me in, and the lads who'll wheel me out. Drink while you can still lift the glass.'
Inscription in a book given to Mrs. Byrne next door: 'For Mrs. B, whose cakes are the one reliable thing on Stannaway Road. — Christy.'

Letter-to-editor excerpt, Irish Press 1974: 'Sir — your article on the new housing for the disabled is well-meaning and utterly useless. We do not need ramps without doorways. We need doorways wide enough to wheel through. Ask us. We'll tell you. — C. Brown, Kimmage.'

Handwritten note to a young disabled correspondent: 'You asked me for advice. Here it is: the world will underestimate you every day. Let it. Then prove it wrong on a Tuesday, when nobody's looking. — C.B.'
Toast at my own fortieth birthday, 1972: 'Forty years I've been in this body. Another forty and I'll be in a box. Drink up, lads — the middle bit is the good bit.'

Stranger (in pub): Are you the writer fella?
Me: I might be.
Stranger: The one with the foot?
Me: I've two feet, but only one that works.
Stranger: Can I buy you a pint?
Me: You can, and may your descendants prosper.

Behan: Brown, you're a fraud.
Me: Behan, you're a drunk.
Behan: I'm a drunk who admits it.
Me: I'm a fraud who writes about it. Fifty-fifty, Brendan.
Behan: Fair enough. Get the next round.

American visitor: Mr. Brown, you're an inspiration.
Me: Am I.
American: Truly. Truly.
Me: I'd rather be a good writer than an inspiration. The first pays better.
American: Oh. I see.
Me: More tea?

Mrs. Byrne: Christy, I've brought a cake.
Me: Mrs. Byrne, you're the love of my life.
Mrs. Byrne: Don't let your wife hear you.
Me: She heard. She's making tea. She approves of cake.
Mrs. Byrne: Good woman, Mary.
Me: She is, God help her.

Journalist: What's it like to be famous, Mr. Brown?
Me: Same as being unfamous, only with more letters to answer.
Journalist: That's very humble.
Me: It's not humble. It's accurate. Humility is a different thing altogether.

Young disabled lad (visiting): Did you ever want to give up?
Me: Every Monday.
Lad: And Tuesday?
Me: By Tuesday I've forgotten why. That's the trick.
Lad: That's not much of a trick.
Me: It's the only one I have. Use it wisely.

Me: Pint of plain, Paddy.
Paddy (publican): Is that wise?
Me: It is not. Pour it anyway.
Paddy: Mary will kill me.
Me: She'll kill me first. You'll have time to escape.
Paddy: Fair.

Priest (visiting): Christy, have you made your peace with God?
Me: We've an understanding, Father. He leaves me to my work, I leave him to his.
Priest: That's not how it's supposed to go.
Me: It's how it's going, all the same. Cup of tea?

Editor (London, visiting): The new book needs a title.
Me: Down All the Days.
Editor: That's the one you're writing now.
Me: Oh. I thought you meant that one.
Editor: I mean the next one.
Me: Give me three weeks and a bottle and I'll have a title for you.

Woman at a party: Is it true you write with your foot?
Me: It's true.
Woman: That must be so hard.
Me: Less hard than writing with my head, which is the part that gives me the most trouble.
Woman: (laughs) You're wicked.
Me: Only on my good days.

Sean: The Americans want to make a film.
Me: They'll not make it.
Sean: They said they would.
Me: They always say. None of them has ever said yes to the money.
Sean: This one might.
Me: Then this one might. I'll believe it when I see my name in lights in Kimmage.

Me: Mary, who was that at the door?
Mary: A man from the Guardian.
Me: What did he want?
Mary: A quote on Ireland.
Me: Tell him Ireland is a grand country, run by fools, populated by drunks, and saved only by its writers and its mothers. Quote me on that.
Mary: I'll edit it down.

Behan: Brown, come to London with me.
Me: In what, Brendan, a shopping trolley?
Behan: We'll think of something.
Me: I can't manage the ferry, you eejit.
Behan: Then I'll bring London to you.
Me: You've brought half of it already in that flask.

Me: Did the lads get home alright last night?
Mary: Sean did. Paddy slept on the floor.
Me: Is he still here?
Mary: In the scullery, snoring like a bull.
Me: Poke him awake, love, and put the kettle on. He'll want tea before the Mass.

Stranger at the bar: You're Christy Brown.
Me: I am.
Stranger: My sister has CP. She reads your book every year.
Me: Tell her to write her own. She'll make a better job of it than I did.
Stranger: She says she can't.
Me: She can. Tell her I said so. That's the whole secret.

Priest: Will I give you a blessing, Christy?
Me: You will, Father, if it costs nothing.
Priest: Blessings are free.
Me: Nothing is free in Dublin, Father, but go on.
Priest: (blesses)
Me: Thank you. Now off with you. The pub opens at half five.

Me: Mary, is that the postman again?
Mary: Three more letters. America, London, Cork.
Me: What do they want?
Mary: Advice, an interview, and a free book.
Me: Send them all a signed postcard that says 'Read, write, and drink less than I do.'
Mary: That's not advice, Christy.
Me: It's the best I have today.

Me: Did you see the review in the Times?
Sean: I did.
Me: What did he say?
Sean: He called you a genius.
Me: And?
Sean: And a drunk.
Me: Fair on both counts. Pour us another, will you.
